title: Correlation: Finding Redundant Columns
concept: Dropping highly correlated features removes redundant information, simplifying the model without losing predictive power.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: In the previous lesson, we learned about the 'Curse of Dimensionality.' Why do too many features (columns) hurt a model?

options.text: They add noise, slow down training, and make the model prone to overfitting
They make the dataset too simple for the model to learn anything
They automatically delete the model's weights

exercises.explain: Right! Too many columns increase noise and complexity, making models slow and less accurate on new data. Now, what if the columns repeat the same info?

cards.title: Reading two identical newspapers
cards.body: If you buy two copies of the same newspaper, you don't get twice as much news. You just waste money. In data science, having columns that tell the exact same story wastes computational power and confuses your model.

cards.title: What is correlation?
cards.body: Correlation measures how closely two features move together. If feature A always goes up when feature B goes up, they are highly correlated and contain redundant information.

cards.title: Temperature scales
cards.body: Imagine a dataset with columns for [Temp_Celsius, Temp_Fahrenheit]. Knowing one tells you the other perfectly. Keeping both in your model is redundant; dropping one makes your model simpler with zero loss of information.

cards.title: Income vs. Tax paid
cards.body: In a financial dataset, [Monthly_Income] and [Income_Tax_Paid] are highly correlated. Instead of keeping both and forcing the model to learn their relationship, we can drop one to keep our model clean and fast.

cards.title: Correlation isn't causation, but...
cards.body: While correlation doesn't mean one feature causes the other, for feature selection, we don't care about cause. If two features are highly correlated, they are mathematically redundant. We drop one purely to simplify the input.

cards.title: Redundant vs. Independent
cards.body: How features relate to each other:

left.text:
Temp in C & Temp in F
Drop one to simplify

exercises.prompt: What happens when you train a model with two features that are 100% correlated?

options.text: You waste computing power on redundant information
The model automatically averages them together
The model's accuracy instantly drops to zero
The dataset becomes twice as large in rows

exercises.explain: Correlated features supply duplicate information, making the model unnecessarily complex and slower.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Dropping one of two highly correlated features will cause your model to lose important information.
exercises.explain: False! Because they tell the same story, the remaining feature holds almost all the predictive power.

exercises.sentence: If two columns move together in a predictable pattern, they are highly ___.
exercises.explain: Correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables.

exercises.prompt: Which pair of features is most likely to be highly correlated?

options.text: Total sales revenue and sales tax collected
A customer's age and their shoe size
The car's weight and the owner's zip code

exercises.explain: Sales tax is a direct percentage of sales revenue, making them extremely correlated.

exercises.prompt: You are predicting car prices. Your columns are: car_weight, engine_size, fuel_efficiency, and weight_in_grams. You notice car_weight and weight_in_grams have a correlation of 1.0. What should you do?

options.text: Drop weight_in_grams because it is completely redundant with car_weight
Keep both to double the mathematical weight of the car
Drop both columns to avoid any correlation in the dataset
Multiply the two columns together to create a new feature

exercises.explain: Splendid! Since they represent the exact same physical property in different units, keeping both is redundant. Dropping weight_in_grams leaves you with a simpler, cleaner dataset. Next: how to drop columns that don't change at all!
